A Practical Guide to Kubernetes Observability

This is a general technical reference for instrumenting workloads running on
Kubernetes. It is not tied to any company, platform, or engineer.

THE THREE SIGNALS

Observability on Kubernetes rests on metrics, logs, and traces. Metrics are
cheap, aggregatable time series — request rate, error rate, and latency
percentiles are the workhorses. Logs are high-cardinality event records, useful
for the specific and the rare. Traces stitch a single request across services so
you can see where latency accrues. Each answers a different question; none
replaces the others.

METRICS WITH PROMETHEUS

Prometheus scrapes an HTTP endpoint on each pod and stores samples locally.
Expose the four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — and
let Prometheus handle aggregation. For retention beyond a few weeks, ship
samples to a long-term store such as Thanos or Cortex. Avoid unbounded label
cardinality; a label per user identifier will eventually exhaust memory.

DASHBOARDS AND ALERTING

Grafana renders Prometheus data into dashboards, but a dashboard nobody watches
at three in the morning is decoration. Alerting rules should fire on
user-visible symptoms — elevated error rate or latency — rather than on causes
like high CPU, which may be perfectly normal. Every alert should point to a
runbook.

DISTRIBUTED TRACING

OpenTelemetry has become the vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation.
Instrument services to emit spans, propagate trace context across network
boundaries, and export to a collector that fans out to a backend such as Jaeger
or Tempo. Sampling keeps the volume manageable: head-based sampling is simple,
tail-based sampling preserves the interesting traces at higher cost.

KUBERNETES-SPECIFIC CONCERNS

Pods are ephemeral, so tie telemetry to stable identifiers — the deployment and
namespace — not the pod name. Scrape kube-state-metrics for cluster-level
object state and the kubelet's cAdvisor endpoint for container resource usage.
Watch for noisy-neighbor saturation on shared nodes, and remember that a
liveness probe that is too aggressive will restart a slow-but-healthy pod and
turn a latency blip into an outage.
